description: Two photos documenting grub damage on Kentucky bluegrass in Indianapolis. Tests correct pest identification and appropriate curative grub treatment recommendations.

photos.caption: Wide view of KBG lawn showing irregular, spongy brown patches that pull up easily from the soil surface, with visible dead turf sections and surrounding healthy grass.
Pulled-back section of dead turf revealing white C-shaped Japanese beetle grub larvae at the soil-thatch interface, approximately 1 inch long.

profile.notes: Large brown patches appeared in August. The dead sections pull up like carpet — no roots attached underneath. Found white grubs when I peeled back the turf.
profile.currentRoutine: Mow at 3 inches weekly, water twice a week. No grub preventive applied this year.

groundTruth.photoNotes: Two photos showing classic grub damage on KBG. First photo shows irregular spongy brown patches with turf lifting freely from soil. Second photo confirms white C-shaped grub larvae at the soil surface — consistent with Japanese beetle or masked chafer grubs at late instar stage.
